coredata.dc:title: A simple and sensitive fluorescent sensor for methyl parathion based on l-tyrosine methyl ester functionalized carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Hou, Juying
Dong, Jing
Zhu, Haishuang
Teng, Xue
Ai, Shiyun
Mang, Minglin
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a simple and sensitive fluorescent sensor for methyl parathion is developed based on l-tyrosine methyl ester functionalized carbon dots (Tyr-CDs) and tyrosinase system. The carbon dots are obtained by simple hydrothermal reaction using citric acid as carbon resource and l-tyrosine methyl ester as modification reagent. The carbon dots are characterized by transmission electron microscope, high resolution transmission electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction spectrum, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The carbon dots show strong and stable photoluminescence with a quantum yield of 3.8%. Tyrosinase can catalyze the oxidation of tyrosine methyl ester on the surface of carbon dots to corresponding quinone products, which can quench the fluorescence of carbon dots. When organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) are introduced in system, they can decrease the enzyme activity, thus decrease the fluorescence quenching rate. Methyl parathion, as a model of OPs, was detected. Experimental results show that the enzyme inhibition rate is proportional to the logarithm of the methyl parathion concentration in the range 1.0×10−10–1.0×10−4 M with the detection limit (S/N=3) of 4.8×10−11 M. This determination method shows a low detection limit, wide linear range, good selectivity and high reproducibility. This sensing system has been successfully used for the analysis of cabbage, milk and fruit juice samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent
Sensor
Organophosphorus compounds
Tyrosinase
Enzyme inhibition

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of MP in phosphate buffer
Measurement and calibration of MP in cabbage, milk and fruit juice
Formation mechanism and characterization of Tyr-CDs
The quenching effect of tyrosinase on the fluorescence of Tyr-CDs
Optimization of the experimental conditions for MP determination
Detection of MP in phosphate buffer
The anti-interference ability, stability and reproducibility
Determination of MP in the cabbage, milk and fruit juice samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagrams of the formation mechanism of Tyr-CDs; TEM micrographs at different magnifications (b) 100nm (with the corresponding particle size distribution histogram, inset) and (c) 20nm; (d) HRTEM image; (e) XRD pattern of Tyr-CDs.

(a) Time-dependent fluorescence intensity of Tyr-CDs in the presence of 20U/mL TYR; (b) effect of pH, (b) ionic strengths (ionic strengths were controlled by various concentrations of NaCl) and (c) temperature on the PL intensity of Tyr-CDs and the quenching rate of TYR. Error bars represent the standard deviations of five independent measurements.
Effect of (a) concentration of enzyme, (b) temperature and (c) breeding time on inhibition rate over time. Error bars represent the standard deviations of five independent measurements.

(a) PL spectra of Tyr-CDs in presence of 20U/mL TYR bred by different concentrations of MP; and (b) calibration plot for detecting MP. Error bars represent the standard deviations of five independent measurements.
Schematic illustration of measuring principle of OPs sensor.

head.ce:title: A simple and sensitive fluorescent sensor for methyl parathion based on l-tyrosine methyl ester functionalized carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agricultural University, Taian, Shandong 271018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agricultural University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Shandong Agricultural University, Taian, Shandong 271018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering, Shandong Agricultural University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a simple and sensitive fluorescent sensor for methyl parathion is developed based on l-tyrosine methyl ester functionalized carbon dots (Tyr-CDs) and tyrosinase system. The carbon dots are obtained by simple hydrothermal reaction using citric acid as carbon resource and l-tyrosine methyl ester as modification reagent. The carbon dots are characterized by transmission electron microscope, high resolution transmission electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction spectrum, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The carbon dots show strong and stable photoluminescence with a quantum yield of 3.8%. Tyrosinase can catalyze the oxidation of tyrosine methyl ester on the surface of carbon dots to corresponding quinone products, which can quench the fluorescence of carbon dots. When organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) are introduced in system, they can decrease the enzyme activity, thus decrease the fluorescence quenching rate. Methyl parathion, as a model of OPs, was detected. Experimental results show that the enzyme inhibition rate is proportional to the logarithm of the methyl parathion concentration in the range 1.0×10−10–1.0×10−4 M with the detection limit (S/N=3) of 4.8×10−11 M. This determination method shows a low detection limit, wide linear range, good selectivity and high reproducibility. This sensing system has been successfully used for the analysis of cabbage, milk and fruit juice samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to their high efficiency for insect elimination, easy synthesis, and low cost, organophosphorus compounds (OPs) have been extensively used as pesticides in modern agriculture, and can be used chemical warfare agents in terrorist attacks or military activities. According to statistics, about a billion pounds of OPs are released globally into the soil, atmosphere, food and water supplies each year, posing a great danger to human health and the ecosystem. The acute toxicity of OPs is associated with their capacity to irreversibly inhibit the activity of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) in the central and peripheral nervous system (Sarabia et al., 2009; Shenouda et al., 2009), resulting in the accumulation of the neurotransmitter acetylcholine in the body, which can lead to organ failure and eventual death. Some kinds of OPs have been forbidden to apply in agricultural products fields due to their high toxicity, such as methyl parathion, methamidophos, parathion, and azodrin. However, the misuse of these OPs in agricultural products still exists in some countries. Therefore, there is an urgent demand for the determination of OPs.

A number of analytical techniques are in use for the detection of OPs, such as chromatography (Becker et al., 1997), electrochemistry (Joshi et al., 2005); field-effect transistors (Singh et al., 1999) and colorimetric assay (Andreou and Clonis, 2002; Liang et al., 2012), etc., fluorescent detection have attracted tremendous attention (Azab et al., 2013; Gao et al., 2012; Meng et al., 2013) owing to high sensitivity, low-cost, and operational simplicity. However, all these above-mentioned methods suffer from some drawbacks in the preparation process of sensors, such as time-consuming and tedious processing steps, severe synthetic conditions, and high cost, etc., which limit their wide applications. Recently, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have attracted tremendous research interest, owing to their outstanding properties as well as facile synthetic routes with abundant and cheap raw materials. Compared to organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, carbon dots possess distinct benefits, such as good water-solubility, high chemical inertness, facile functionalization, low photobleaching, low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility. Carbon dots have been widely applied in many areas such as bioimaging (Fowley et al., 2012), catalysis (Li et al., 2010; Zhu et al., 2012), and sensors (He et al., 2014; Shen and Xia, 2014; Zong et al., 2014). The applications of CDs in chemical sensing have been elaborated in the latest review articles (Lim et al., 2014; Lin et al., 2014). The chemical sensing based on CDs mainly includes the detections of metal ions and inorganic anions, such as Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Cr(VI), Ag+,
C 2
O 4
2 -
, PO4 3−, CN−, F−, ClO− and I−.

In this paper, we prepared l-tyrosine methyl ester functionalized carbon dots (Tyr-CDs) by simple hydrothermal reaction of citric acid and l-tyrosine methyl ester and successfully constructed one kind of methyl parathion (MP) sensor. The sensor is based on the following principles: (1) The tyrosine methyl ester can be transformed into the corresponding quinone products in presence of tyrosinase and O2; (2) As one kind of electron acceptor, the quinone products linked to CDs can quench the fluorescence of CDs; (3) MP can inhibit the activity of tyrosinase, thus inhibit the quenching of fluorescence. The linear range of the MP sensor is 1.0×10−10–1.0×10−4 M. The established biosensors could be used for determination of different kinds of OPs.

Citric acid was purchased from Tianjin Basf Chemical Co., Ltd. (China). l-Tyrosine methyl ester hydrochloride, NaH2PO4, and Na2HPO4 were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Tyrosinase (TYR, EC 1.14.18.1, from mushroom, lyophilized powder) and OPs were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). All chemicals were used as received without further purification. The water used throughout all the experiments was purified through redistillation process. The cabbage, milk and fruit juice samples were obtained from a local supermarket.

Citric acid (0.5g), l-tyrosine methyl ester hydrochloride (0.25g) was mixed with H2O (10mL), then added into a 40mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200°C for 5h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting brown solution was centrifuged at 3000rpm for 15min to remove the large dots. Then, the solution was extensively dialyzed against redistilled water in a dialysis membrane (2000MW CO) for 3d. By freeze-dried method, brown powder was obtained for further characterization. The yield was calculated to be ca. 3.2%. The powder was dispersed in distilled water at a concentration of 1mg/mL for further use.

The morphology and microstructures of Tyr-CDs were analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, Tecnai G2 F20, USA) operated at 200kV. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were obtained on a Thermo Nicolet-380 IR spectrophotometer (USA) with the KBr pellet technique ranging from 400 to 4000cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was investigated using Al Kα excitation source (1486.6eV) in a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI apparatus (USA). Fluorescence spectroscopy was performed with a Cary Eclipse spectrophotometer (USA, VARIAN). UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained using a UV-2450 Shimadzu Vis-spectrometer (Japan). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) data were collected using a Rigaku DLMAX-2550V diffractometer (40kV, Cu Ka (k=1.54056Å); scan speed of 6°/min).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of MP in phosphate buffer
ce:section.ce:para: The procedure for MP determination was as follows: (1) TYR was first incubated with 0.01M Phosphate Buffer (PBS, pH=7.0) containing different concentrations of MP at certain temperature for a period of time; (2) Tyr-CDs was added in the above solution and the fluorescence signal was monitored over time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement and calibration of MP in cabbage, milk and fruit juice
ce:section.ce:para: The samples were first pretreated. 0.2kg of cabbage was spiked with different quantities of MP. After overnight, the cabbage sample was extracted with 30mL absolute ethanol in an ultrasonic bath for 30min. Then, the samples were centrifuged at 3000rpm for 10min, and the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22-μm membrane. At last, the extracts were diluted with 0.01M PBS (pH=7.0). These milk and fruit juice samples were first filtered through a 0.22-μm membrane, and mixed with an equal volume of 2mM PBS (pH=7.0) to ensure the pH value of the solution. Then, different quantities of MP were added into the solution. MP determination procedure in these samples was described as follows: (1) TYR were added into the solution and incubated at 35°C for 9min; (2) Tyr-CDs were added in the above solution and the fluorescence signal was monitored over time. The results were compared with the calibration curve, and the MP concentration could be obtained. The chromatography determination of MP in these samples was carried out according to previously reported method (Berijani et al., 2006) in order to compare the results of two methods. The GC method was based on dispersive liquid–liquid microextraction coupled with gas chromatography–flame photometric detection. A mixture of 12.0μL chlorobenzene and 1.00mL acetone was rapidly injected into the 5.00mL pretreated samples by syringe. Then, the sample was centrifuged for 2min at 5000rpm, the sedimented phase was used for the GC determination of MP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation mechanism and characterization of Tyr-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of Tyr-CDs was completed by the hydrothermal reaction of l-tyrosine methyl ester and citric acid. The raw materials are selected owing to following reasons: (1) As one kind of cheap organic acid, citric acid has been reported as carbon source for carbon dots (Guo et al., 2013; Zhu et al., 2013) with excellent fluorescence, and has carboxylic group, which can react with amino group of tyrosine methyl ester; (2) As one kind of reducing agent, citric acid can prevent tyrosine methyl ester from oxidation under hydrothermal condition; (3) Tyrosine methyl ester, rather than tyrosine, is chosen as modification agent of CDs to reduce the intermolecular reaction of tyrosine, which maybe results that the tyrosine loses the ability to form the quinone products. The exact formation mechanism of Tyr-CDs has not yet been confirmed. Maybe, it contains two kinds of different process. One possible process is that citric acid and l-tyrosine methyl ester first polymerize to form bonds of amide and ester, etc, and then the polymer carbonizes to form the Tyr-CDs. Another probable process is that citric acid first carbonizes to form carboxyl CDs, and then the amidation reaction of the carboxyl CDs and l-tyrosine methyl ester takes place to modify the CDs. The existence of tyrosine methyl ester on the surface of CDs can be proved by FTIR, XPS and fluorescence quenching experiments.

The TEM results show that the Tyr-CDs are well-dispersed spherical dots ( Fig. 1b and c) with average diameters of 3.7nm (based on statistical analysis of more than 150 dots). The HRTEM image of Tyr-CDs shows lattice spacing of 0.23nm, which may be attributable to the (100) facet of graphite (Hu et al., 2009; Lu et al., 2009; Qin et al., 2013). The corresponding dot size distribution histogram (Fig. 1b, inset) indicates that these nanodots are mainly distributed in the range of 3–5nm. Fig. 1e represents typical XRD profiles of Tyr-CDs. A broad peak centered at 21.3° (0.42nm), which is close to the (002) facet of graphite. The corresponding interlayer spacing in graphite (0.34nm) becomes larger in Tyr-CDs, which can be attributed to the introduction of more oxygen containing groups.

The FTIR studies are carried about to obtain chemical and structural information about the Tyr-CDs (Fig. S1a, in the Supplementary materials). The results are consistent with the viewpoint of the existence of l-Tyrosine methyl ester. The absorption peak around 3028cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration C=C–H, The absorption peaks at 1607, 1513, and 1441cm−1 are ascribed to the C=C stretching vibration of aromatic hydrocarbon. The absorption peak at 1380cm−1 is assigned to C–H bending vibration of methyl. The absorption peak at 1235cm−1 corresponds to C–O stretching vibration of phenol. The absorption peak at 834cm−1 can be attributed to C–H out-of-plane bending vibration of opposite-substituted aromatic hydrocarbon. The characteristic absorption band of O–H around 3433cm−1 and the stretching vibration band of C=O at 1689cm−1 indicate the presence of carboxylic acid and other oxygen-containing functional groups. The presence of plentiful hydrophilic groups on the surface endows the Tyr-CDs with high water solubility.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy is used to investigate the surface states of the Tyr-CDs. The survey spectrum (Fig. S2a, in the Supplementary materials) of the Tyr-CDs shows three typical peaks of C1s, N1s and O1s. The spectrum of C1s (Fig. S2b, in the Supplementary materials) can be deconvoluted into several single peaks that correspond to C=C (284.5eV), C=C (285.1eV), C–N (285.7eV), C–O (286.2eV) and C=O (288.9eV) functional groups (Liu et al., 2012). The N1s spectrum (Fig. S2c, in the Supplementary materials) reveals three relative nitrogen species of C–N–C (399.7eV), N–C3 (400.6eV), and N–H (401.3eV) (Li et al., 2013). The spectrum of O1s (Fig. S2d, in the Supplementary materials) further confirms these observations with two characteristic oxygen states of C=O (531.4eV) and C–O (532.2eV) (Liu et al., 2012). These results confirm once again that the Tyr-CDs are functionalized with plentiful hydrophilic groups.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. S3a, in the Supplementary materials) shows a typical characteristic of fluorescent CDs around ca. 272nm, which is ascribed to the typical absorption of an aromatic π system or the n−p* transition of the carbonyl (Li et al., 2011; Zhu et al., 2012). Fig. S3b (see Supplementary materials) shows photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of the Tyr-CDs prepared with different quantities l-tyrosine methyl ester at 348nm excitation. When the l-tyrosine methyl ester is 0.25g, peak width at half height of the fluorescent peak is the smallest, so 0.25g is chosen in the experiment. Fig. S4 (see Supplementary materials) shows the emission spectra at different excitation wavelength and the excitation spectra at 433nm emission. In view of emission wavelength and intensity, the excitation wavelength was fixed at 348nm. When the excitation wavelength increased from 348 nm, the emission wavelength correspondingly increased. Meanwhile, the intensity of PL decreased. The emission of Tyr-CDs at 348nm excitation is centered at 433nm. The PL emission wavelength of Tyr-CDs is close to that of other reported CDs (Anilkumar et al., 2013, Lin et al., 2013, Shen and Xia, 2014), which have successfully used as sensors. The quantum yield is calculated to be 3.8% at 348nm excitation (quantum yield calculations, see Supplementary Materials). The exact mechanisms of PL responsible for carbon nanomaterials have not yet been confirmed. Some kinds of mechanisms can attribute to the fluorescence of carbon nanomaterials, such as excitons of carbon, emissive traps, the quantum confinement effect, aromatic structures, oxygen-containing groups, edge defects, and free zigzag sites, etc (Bao et al., 2011; Li et al., 2010; Loh et al., 2010; Mochalin and Gogotsi, 2009; Zhou et al., 2007).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The quenching effect of tyrosinase on the fluorescence of Tyr-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 illustrates the fluorescence switch mechanism for the detection of OPs based on Tyr-CDs. The units of tyrosine methyl ester on the surface of CDs can be oxidized to corresponding quinone units. As a kind of electron acceptor, the quinone units on the surface of CDs can result in the quenching the fluorescence of CDs. OPs can inhibit the activity of TYR, thus inhibit the quenching of fluorescence. Based on the above principle, the OPs sensor was constructed.

Fig. 2a shows a typical time-dependent fluorescence intensity of Tyr-CDs after mixed with 20U/mL TYR in 0.01M PBS buffer (pH=7) at 35°C. As the time was prolonged, the fluorescence intensity decreased gradually with reaction time up to 6min and then leveled off. The phenomenon indicated that the Tyr-CDs can be used as sensor for determination of TYR and its inhibitors. When the reaction time was fixed at 6min, other influences on quenching efficiency were studied. Fig. 2b–d shows the effect of pH, salting strength, and temperature on the fluorescence quenching of Tyr-CDs in the presence of TYR (I 0 and I stand for the PL intensity of Tyr-CDs before and after quenching experiment, respectively). From Fig. 2b, we can see that pH can influence the fluorescence intensity and the quenching efficiency, the pH 7 PBS was chosen in the following experiments for the higher fluorescence intensity and the highest quenching efficiency. From Fig. 2c, we can see that the influence of salting strength on the fluorescence intensity and the quenching efficiency can be ignored, so no extra salts were added into the PBS. Fig. 2d shows the effect of temperature on the fluorescence intensity, 35°C was chosen as the reaction temperature for the highest quenching efficiency. These results show that pH=7 and 35°C is appropriate for the enzyme reaction, which is consistent with the optimum condition for TYR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the experimental conditions for MP determination
ce:section.ce:para:
As a model of organphosphorus pesticides, methyl parathion is chosen as the inhibitor of TYR. Fig. S5 (see Supplementary materials) shows PL spectra of 1.0×10−4 M MP at 348nm excitation, the PL emission can be ignored. The sensitivity of the MP sensor is related to many factors, such as the pH of solution, the concentration of enzyme, the reaction temperature and the incubation time of MP and TYR, so the experimental conditions are optimized for determination of MP. In optimization test, the concentration of MP adopted was 1.0×10−8 M. We use the inhibition rate of MP (I%) as a signal for the detection of MP. I% was calculated using Eq. (1),
(1)
I % =
F MP
−
F TYR
F 0
−
F TYR
× 100 %
where F 0 is the initial fluorescence intensity without TYR and MP, F TYR stands for the fluorescence intensity after quenching of TYR, F MP is the fluorescence intensity after the MP inhibition reaction and TYR quenching experiment.

From Fig. 3a and b, we can see that the reaction time of TYR and Tyr-CDs has remarkable influence on the inhibition rate. The inhibition rate first increased gradually with reaction time and then decreased. These results were owing to following reasons: (1) No matter whether MP was introduced into the reaction system or not, the quenching percentage of TYR first increased with time and then leveled off; (2) When the reaction in the system without MP introduced was most complete, the PL intensity of this system keep constant, however the reaction in the system with MP introduced still take place, which give rise to the decrease of the inhibition rate of MP. So, the reaction time in the determination experiment was strictly controlled.

The influence of the enzyme concentration on inhibition rate is shown in Fig. 3a. TYR concentrations adopted in this study was 15, 20, and 25U/ml. The incubating time and temperature was fixed. The results clearly indicated that the optimum concentration of TYR was 20U/ml. When the time was 5min, the maximum inhibition rate of 0.2830 was obtained using 20U/ml TYR. When the TYR concentration was 15 and 25U/ml, the inhibition rate significantly decreased. These results were owing to following reasons: (1) When the TYR concentration was 15U/ml, the quenching percentage of TYR was lower, which leaded to the low inhibition rate; (2) When the TYR concentration was 25U/ml, the TYR was excessive, thus a low inhibition rate of MP was obtained.

The influence of the incubating and reaction temperature on inhibition rate is shown in Fig. 3b. The temperature adopted in this study was 25 35 and 45°C. Other experimental conditions were fixed. The results clearly indicated that the maximum inhibition rate was obtained at 35°C when the time was 5min. This phenomenon indicated that the high activity of TYR under the optimum temperature was in favor of high inhibition rate.

The influence of the incubating time on inhibition rate is shown in Fig. 3c. The TYR concentration was 20U/ml, and incubating temperature was 35°C. From Fig. 4c, it could be seen that the inhibition rate increased gradually to a maximum value with the increasing of incubating time to 9min and then leveled off. This phenomenon demonstrated that the combination of TYR and MP was saturated when the incubating time was 9min.

In a word, the optimal determination conditions were as follows: first, 20U/ml of TYR was first incubated by MP in pH 7 PBS at 35°C for 9min; second, Tyr-CDs was added into the solution, and the mixed solution reacted at 35°C for 5min; final the PL spectrum of the mixed solution was recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of MP in phosphate buffer
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimum experimental conditions, calibration plots were generated for MP (Fig. 4). The results exhibited a good linear relationship between the inhibition rate and log CMP. The semi-log dependence of inhibition rate on the OPs concentration has been found in previous reported OPs sensing system based on enzyme inhibition mechanism (Gao et al., 2012; Liang et al., 2012; Zheng et al., 2011). The inhibition rate increased with log CMP in the range of 1.0×10−10–1.0×10−4 M. The regression equation was I (%)=120.25+11.36log C MP (R 2=0.997). The detection limit was estimated to be 4.8×10−11 M (1.3×10−5 ppm) at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3, which was much lower than the lowest value (0.01ppm) of the maximum residue limits (MRLs) of other OPs as reported in the European Union pesticides database as well as those from the U.S. Department Agriculture. Table S1 (see the Supplementary materials) shows the comparison of the different fluorescent probes for OPs detection, suggesting the sensitivity of our sensing system is comparable to that of previously reported sensing systems. Fig. S6 (see the Supplementary materials) further compared the inhibition rate of three kinds of OPs at the concentration of 1.0×10−8 mol/L. It can be seen that the inhibition rate were 28.30, 33.23 and 24.28 for MP, paraoxon and chlorpyrifos, respectively. These results suggested that the established biosensors could be used for determination of different kinds of OPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The anti-interference ability, stability and reproducibility
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the proposed method for MP detection was further evaluated with various coexistence substances added. Fig. S7 (see the Supplementary materials) shows the interference effect of inorganic ions, sugar, and organic acids on the determination of MP. As shown in Fig. S7 (see the Supplementary materials), the results suggested that 106-fold for NaCl and KCl, 400-fold for CaCl2 Zn(Ac)2, FeCl3, and CuSO4, 200-fold for ascorbic acid, 104-fold for glucose, sodium acetate and sodium citrate, 200-fold for glycerol had no influence on the signals of 1.0×10−7 M MP with deviations below 5%. These results show that there was little interference from commonly existed substances. Thus, the new sensitive method displays high selectivity for the determination of MP.

The stability and reproducibility of this sensor system were evaluated by determination of 1.0×10−7 M MP. The sensor retained 97.7% of its initial response after the Tyr-CDs was kept in refrigerator at 4°C for 30 days, which indicated that the sensor had good stability. The reproducibility was investigated by five parallel prepared Tyr-CDs. The relative standard deviation was 3.3%, which suggested that the sensor displayed good reproducibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of MP in the cabbage, milk and fruit juice samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the accuracy, repeatability and selectivity of the proposed method, different concentrations of MP in the cabbage, milk and fruit juice samples were determined and the results were listed in Table S2 (see the Supplementary materials). From Table S2 (see the Supplementary materials), it can be seen that the recoveries of the three kinds of samples were found to be in the range 98–104%, and the RSDs were less than 6.0%. The recoveries of our sensing system were comparable to that of previously reported chromatography method. The results revealed that it was an applicable method for organophosphate compound-contaminated environmental samples.

In conclusion, we have prepared l-tyrosine methyl ester functionalized carbon dots by simple hydrothermal reaction. The existence of l-tyrosine methyl ester on the surface of carbon dots can be proved by FTIR, XPS, and fluorescence quenching experiments. The modification and formation of carbon dots was completed by one step reaction. Compared to step-by-step synthesis-modification, one step preparation possesses many advantages, such as time-saving, low cost and less pollution, etc. The CDs can be used as a sensitive and selective MP sensor based on enzyme inhibition. This sensing system has been successfully used for the analysis of cabbage, milk and fruit juice samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21375079, 51402175), the National Scientific and Technological Support Project of China (No. 2012BAK17B 05) and Project of Development of Science and Technology of Shandong Province, China (No. 2013GZX20109).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2014.12.037.
